title: Novo Nordisk Partners with OpenAI to Accelerate Drug Discovery for Diabetes and Obesity
detail: Novo Nordisk, the world's leading diabetes and obesity drug maker, announced on April 14 a strategic partnership with OpenAI to integrate advanced AI throughout its drug development pipeline. CEO Mike Doustdar stated the collaboration enables the company to 'analyse datasets at a scale that was previously impossible,' dramatically accelerating the identification of drug candidates for chronic diseases like diabetes and obesity — conditions affecting over 800 million people globally. The partnership will apply OpenAI's large language and reasoning models to molecular biology datasets, clinical trial data analysis, and biomarker identification. This follows a trend of major pharmaceutical companies forming AI-first drug discovery alliances, though the Novo Nordisk-OpenAI partnership is notable for targeting diseases affecting primarily lower- and middle-income countries and populations in the Global South where affordable treatment remains inaccessible.

media.caption: Novo Nordisk and OpenAI partner to use AI for accelerating drug discovery for chronic disease

title: U.S. Department of Education Finalizes AI as Priority in Grant Programs — Expanding AI Literacy Nationally
detail: The U.S. Department of Education finalized rules on April 14 establishing artificial intelligence as a priority area for its discretionary grant programs to school districts and higher education institutions. The grant priorities include: integrating AI literacy into K-12 curricula, expanding computer science education access in underserved communities, embedding responsible AI use in teacher preparation programs, and funding AI-powered personalized learning tools for students with disabilities. The rules direct significant federal education funding toward AI capacity-building, requiring grantees to address ethical AI implementation, algorithmic bias awareness, and student data privacy. Advocates for educational equity noted the explicit focus on Title I schools and rural districts, historically excluded from technology adoption funding, as a significant equity provision.

media.caption: U.S. Department of Education finalizes AI grant priorities for K-12 schools and colleges
